summary: Gruber argues that Flash's performance problems on mobile devices, while real, are a distraction from the fundamental issue: Apple opposes Flash because it represents someone else's proprietary platform over which Apple has no control. He contends that Apple values the open web not for ideological reasons but because open standards allow Apple to control its own implementation through WebKit while no single competitor can lock them out. Gruber draws a parallel between Apple and Google, noting both companies have found ways to bet with the web rather than against it. He dismisses fears that Apple might someday oppose the web itself, arguing that the web as the lowest common denominator platform actually benefits Apple enormously compared to the Windows era. The only path for Flash onto iPhone OS would require it to succeed on competing platforms and demonstrably shift consumer demand away from Apple devices.
key_points: Flash's performance issues give Apple a convenient apolitical excuse, but the real reason is control — Apple won't accept a key platform component it doesn't own
Apple's strategy is to control implementation (WebKit) while embracing open standards, ensuring they prosper regardless of how popular the web becomes
The web replacing Windows as the lowest common denominator platform is arguably the best thing that ever happened to Apple
Both Apple and Google bet with the open web for self-interested reasons — Apple controls the browser, Google controls search and ads
Flash can only reach iPhone OS if it first succeeds on competing platforms and shifts consumer demand away from Apple devices
money_quotes: Even if Adobe solves Flash's performance problems, I still doubt Apple will want to include it in iPhone OS.
It's a way for Apple to argue that they can't rather than that they won't.
Apple isn't a web company, but the web might be the best thing that ever happened to them.
The weird thing about a completely open platform based on open standards is that while no single vendor, such as Apple, can control the content or the standards, it can control its implementation.
What Apple doesn't want is someone else's proprietary platform, where they have no control at all. That's what Flash is.
Google and Apple have found different ways to bet with the web, rather than against the web.
There's no bug Apple can't try to fix on their own. No performance problem they can't try to tackle. No one they need to wait for.
key_insight: Apple's opposition to Flash isn't about performance — it's about refusing to let a critical layer of their platform be controlled by another company, which is why they embrace the open web: no single competitor can own it, but Apple can own the best implementation of it.
